aws-architect.title: Cloud and AWS Fundamentals
aws-architect.description: Learn the core concepts of cloud computing and the AWS global infrastructure. Understand regions, availability zones, and the shared responsibility model.
topics: Cloud computing models IaaS PaaS SaaS

resources.label: AWS Certified Cloud Practitioner Full Course

aws-architect.challenge: Create an EC2 instance, attach an IAM role with S3 read access, and upload a file to S3 from the instance using the CLI.

children.title: IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS Explained
children.description: Understanding the three cloud service models is essential because it determines your level of responsibility, control, and operational overhead. IaaS gives you raw infrastructure like EC2, PaaS abstracts the OS and runtime like Elastic Beanstalk, and SaaS delivers fully managed software. Choosing the wrong model for a workload leads to unnecessary complexity or insufficient control.

resources.label: IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS – IBM

children.title: AWS Global Infrastructure
children.description: AWS operates across Regions, Availability Zones, and Edge Locations, and each plays a distinct role in designing resilient architectures. Regions are geographically isolated to protect against large-scale disasters, while AZs within a region provide redundancy at the data-center level. Knowing where to place workloads across this infrastructure is the foundation of every high-availability design.

children.title: Users, Groups, Roles, and Policies
children.description: IAM is the gatekeeper for every AWS API call, making it the single most important service to master early. Users represent human identities, roles represent machine or cross-account identities, and policies define what actions are allowed on which resources. Misconfigured IAM is the root cause of a large proportion of cloud security incidents.

children.title: Shared Responsibility Model
children.description: The shared responsibility model defines exactly which security tasks AWS handles and which fall to you as the customer. AWS secures the underlying hardware, networking, and managed service software, while you are responsible for OS patching, application code, IAM configuration, and data encryption. Failing to understand this boundary leads to false assumptions about what is secured by default.

children.title: EC2 Instance Types and Pricing
children.description: EC2 is the backbone of compute on AWS, and selecting the right instance type is critical for both performance and cost efficiency. Instance families are optimized for different workload profiles: compute-optimized (C series), memory-optimized (R series), and general-purpose (M/T series). Choosing an oversized instance wastes money, while an undersized one degrades application performance under load.

children.title: S3 Buckets and Storage Classes
children.description: Amazon S3 is AWS's most versatile storage service, used for everything from static website hosting to data lake foundations. Understanding its storage classes—Standard, Intelligent-Tiering, Glacier, and others—allows you to dramatically reduce storage costs by automatically moving data to cheaper tiers as access frequency drops. S3 is also the central target for logs, backups, and artifacts across virtually every AWS architecture.

children.title: AWS CLI and Console Basics
children.description: Proficiency with the AWS CLI enables automation, scripting, and repeatable operations that the console alone cannot provide. Every DevOps pipeline and IaC tool ultimately calls AWS APIs, and understanding the CLI structure makes debugging broken deployments far faster. Starting with CLI from day one builds habits that prevent console-only thinking.

resources.label: AWS CLI Tutorial for Beginners – YouTube

children.title: AWS Well-Architected Framework
children.description: The Well-Architected Framework's five pillars—Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, and Cost Optimization—serve as the professional standard for evaluating AWS architectures. AWS Solutions Architects use this framework in formal reviews to identify risks before they become production incidents. Learning it early shapes the way you think about every design decision going forward.

aws-architect.title: Networking in AWS
aws-architect.description: Design and build virtual private clouds with public and private subnets. Configure routing, security groups, and network ACLs to control traffic flow.
aws-architect.challenge: Build a 3-tier VPC with public subnets for load balancers, private subnets for app servers, and isolated subnets for databases with correct routing.

children.title: CIDR Blocks and Subnet Planning
children.description: Proper CIDR block allocation at VPC creation is irreversible without significant rework, making it one of the most consequential early decisions in an AWS architecture. Planning subnets across multiple AZs with enough IP address space for future growth prevents painful IP exhaustion scenarios when you scale. A well-designed CIDR hierarchy also makes route table management and firewall rules far simpler.

children.title: Public vs Private Subnets
children.description: Separating workloads into public and private subnets is the foundation of network security in AWS—internet-facing resources sit in public subnets while databases and application servers are isolated in private ones. Traffic from private subnets exits through a NAT Gateway which provides outbound connectivity without exposing the resource to inbound internet traffic. This separation limits the blast radius of any security breach.

children.title: Internet Gateways
children.description: An Internet Gateway is the VPC component that enables communication between your VPC and the public internet, and without it no resource in any subnet—public or private—can reach the internet. It is horizontally scaled and highly available by design, so it does not become a bottleneck. Understanding its routing requirements—specifically the 0.0.0.0/0 route in the public subnet route table—is required for every internet-connected architecture.

children.title: NAT Gateways and NAT Instances
children.description: NAT Gateways allow private subnet resources to initiate outbound internet connections for software updates and API calls without exposing them to inbound traffic. Choosing between managed NAT Gateways (highly available, higher cost) and NAT Instances (more configurable, single point of failure) directly impacts both availability and monthly spend. For production architectures one NAT Gateway per AZ is required to avoid cross-AZ data transfer costs and single-AZ failure risk.

resources.label: NAT Gateway vs NAT Instance – Medium

children.title: Security Groups vs Network ACLs
children.description: Security Groups are stateful firewalls that operate at the instance level and are the primary mechanism for controlling network access in AWS. Network ACLs are stateless and operate at the subnet boundary, providing a coarser second layer of defence for blocking specific IP ranges. Understanding the difference prevents common mistakes like forgetting return traffic rules in NACLs that inadvertently break connectivity.

children.title: Route Tables and Routing Rules
children.description: Every subnet in a VPC is associated with a route table that determines where traffic is directed based on destination CIDR. Incorrect route table configuration is one of the most common causes of connectivity failures in AWS networking exercises. The most-specific-route rule means that a /32 host route will always win over a /0 default route, which is critical knowledge for VPN and Direct Connect configurations.

children.title: VPC Peering
children.description: VPC peering allows private connectivity between two VPCs without traffic traversing the public internet, enabling resource sharing across accounts and regions. Peering is not transitive—if A peers with B and B peers with C, A cannot reach C—which forces architects to think about full mesh designs or Transit Gateway for more than a few VPCs. Overlapping CIDR blocks cannot be peered, reinforcing the importance of careful CIDR planning from the start.

children.title: VPC Endpoints
children.description: VPC Endpoints allow your private resources to communicate with AWS services like S3 and DynamoDB without traversing the internet, improving security and eliminating data transfer charges through the NAT Gateway. Gateway endpoints are free and cover S3 and DynamoDB, while interface endpoints (powered by PrivateLink) cost money but support dozens of other AWS services. Using endpoints for high-volume AWS API traffic can produce substantial monthly savings on NAT Gateway data processing fees.

resources.label: AWS PrivateLink and VPC Endpoints – YouTube

aws-architect.title: Compute and Auto Scaling
aws-architect.description: Architect scalable and highly available compute layers using EC2 auto scaling groups and elastic load balancers. Understand horizontal scaling patterns and health checks.
topics: Application Load Balancer vs Network Load Balancer
aws-architect.challenge: Deploy a web application across two availability zones using an ALB, auto scaling group, and a custom scaling policy that triggers at 60 percent CPU.

children.title: Launch Templates and AMIs
children.description: Launch Templates are the modern replacement for Launch Configurations and are required for using advanced EC2 features like mixed instance types and Spot capacity pools in Auto Scaling Groups. A well-crafted AMI (Amazon Machine Image) baked with your application and dependencies reduces launch time dramatically compared to installing software via user data on every boot. The combination of a hardened, pre-baked AMI and a declarative Launch Template is the production standard for immutable infrastructure on EC2.

children.title: Placement Groups
children.description: Placement groups control the physical placement of EC2 instances relative to each other, giving architects fine-grained control over latency and fault tolerance. Cluster placement groups pack instances onto the same underlying hardware for the lowest possible network latency, useful for HPC workloads, while Spread placement groups force instances onto distinct hardware racks for maximum fault isolation. Choosing the wrong placement group type for a workload profile can either bottleneck performance or create unnecessary blast radius.

children.title: Scaling Policies and Strategies
children.description: Auto Scaling policies determine how and when your fleet grows and shrinks in response to demand, directly impacting both availability and cost. Target tracking policies are the simplest and most reliable for CPU and request-rate-based scaling, while step scaling provides finer control over response aggressiveness. Setting appropriate cooldown periods prevents over-scaling oscillation where the fleet scales out, waits, scales in, and then needs to scale out again immediately.

resources.label: Auto Scaling Deep Dive – AWS Blog

children.title: Spot Instances in Auto Scaling
children.description: Spot Instances offer discounts of up to 90 percent off On-Demand pricing, making them transformative for batch workloads, stateless web services, and CI/CD runners. The key to reliable Spot usage is diversifying across multiple instance types and AZs so AWS always has capacity to fulfil your request. Implementing Spot Interruption Notices via the EC2 instance metadata service gives your application a two-minute warning to drain gracefully before the instance is reclaimed.

children.title: Application Load Balancer
children.description: The Application Load Balancer (ALB) operates at Layer 7 and is the right choice for HTTP/HTTPS workloads that need content-based routing, host-header routing, and built-in support for WebSockets and HTTP/2. Its native integration with AWS WAF, Cognito, and Lambda targets makes it the foundation of modern web application architectures. ALB access logs are a critical source of data for debugging latency and error patterns when applications misbehave under load.

resources.label: ALB vs NLB – When to Use Which

children.title: Target Groups and Health Checks
children.description: Target groups define the pool of instances or Lambda functions that receive traffic from a load balancer, and their health check settings control when instances are considered ready to serve requests. A poorly tuned health check with an overly aggressive interval can mark healthy instances as unhealthy during initialization, causing thrashing in an Auto Scaling Group. Setting the health check grace period to match your application startup time prevents premature instance termination immediately after launch.

children.title: Reserved Instances vs Savings Plans
children.description: Committing to EC2 capacity through Reserved Instances or Savings Plans reduces compute costs by up to 72 percent compared to On-Demand pricing and is the single largest cost optimization lever for stable workloads. Compute Savings Plans offer the most flexibility by applying discounts automatically to any EC2 instance regardless of region, family, or OS, while Reserved Instances offer slightly deeper discounts in exchange for committing to a specific instance type. The right mix of commitment instruments depends on how predictable your workload shape is over a one-to-three-year horizon.

children.title: Warm Pools for Faster Scaling
children.description: EC2 Auto Scaling Warm Pools allow you to pre-initialize a set of instances in a stopped or running state so they are ready to serve traffic immediately when the fleet needs to scale out, eliminating the latency of bootstrapping a cold instance. This is especially valuable for applications with multi-minute initialization times where a sudden spike in traffic would otherwise take too long to absorb. Warm Pool instances in the stopped state still incur EBS charges but not EC2 charges, making them cheaper than maintaining running standby instances.

aws-architect.title: Storage and Database Services
aws-architect.description: Select appropriate storage and database solutions for different workload requirements. Understand consistency models, replication, and backup strategies.
aws-architect.challenge: Design a data tier with RDS Aurora in multi-AZ mode plus a DynamoDB table for session storage, with automated backups and a read replica for reporting.

children.title: RDS Multi-AZ and Read Replicas
children.description: RDS Multi-AZ deploys a synchronous standby in a second Availability Zone that automatically takes over within 60–120 seconds if the primary fails, providing durability without any application changes. Read Replicas use asynchronous replication to offload read traffic from the primary, improving performance for read-heavy workloads like reporting and analytics. Understanding the difference—Multi-AZ for availability, Read Replicas for scalability—prevents architects from misusing one as a substitute for the other.

resources.label: RDS Multi-AZ vs Read Replicas Explained

children.title: Aurora Serverless and Global Databases
children.description: Amazon Aurora delivers up to 5x the throughput of standard MySQL and 3x PostgreSQL at a fraction of the cost, with a distributed storage layer that automatically replicas data across three AZs. Aurora Serverless v2 auto-scales capacity in fine-grained increments in response to traffic, making it ideal for variable and unpredictable workloads. Aurora Global Databases replicate across up to five regions with sub-second lag, enabling disaster recovery with RPO of under one second for globally distributed applications.

children.title: DynamoDB Tables, Keys, and Indexes
children.description: DynamoDB achieves single-digit millisecond performance at any scale because it routes every request to a specific partition using the partition key, so choosing a high-cardinality partition key is the most important design decision. Global Secondary Indexes allow you to query on non-key attributes at the cost of eventual consistency and additional storage and write capacity. The single-table design pattern, where all entity types coexist in one table differentiated by key prefixes, is the access-pattern-first approach that experts use to minimize cost and latency.

resources.label: DynamoDB Single Table Design – Rick Houlihan

children.title: DynamoDB Streams and Change Data Capture
children.description: DynamoDB Streams capture a time-ordered sequence of every item-level modification in a table, enabling event-driven architectures where downstream Lambda functions react to data changes in near real time. This pattern is commonly used to replicate data to Elasticsearch for search, trigger analytics pipelines, or maintain audit logs without polling the database. Streams retain records for 24 hours and can be consumed by Lambda event source mappings with automatic batching and retry logic.

children.title: EBS Volume Types and Snapshots
children.description: EBS volumes are the persistent block storage attached to EC2 instances, and choosing the right volume type directly affects both IOPS throughput and cost. gp3 volumes are the modern default, offering configurable IOPS and throughput independent of size and replacing the older gp2 model. EBS Snapshots are incremental backups stored in S3 and are the cornerstone of any EC2 backup strategy, enabling point-in-time restores and cross-region DR copies.

children.title: EFS Shared File System
children.description: Amazon EFS provides a fully managed NFS file system that can be mounted simultaneously by thousands of EC2 instances across multiple AZs, enabling shared file access patterns that block storage cannot support. It auto-scales from gigabytes to petabytes without pre-provisioning capacity, and EFS Intelligent Tiering automatically moves files between Standard and Infrequent Access storage classes to optimize cost. EFS is the right choice when multiple instances need to read and write the same files, such as content management systems, shared configuration, or machine learning training data.

resources.label: EFS vs EBS vs S3 – When to Use Each

children.title: S3 Lifecycle Policies and Versioning
children.description: S3 Lifecycle policies automate cost optimization by transitioning objects to cheaper storage tiers or expiring them entirely based on age, preventing storage from growing unbounded. Enabling S3 Versioning protects against accidental deletions and overwrites by retaining all versions of every object, and is a prerequisite for features like Cross-Region Replication and MFA Delete. Combining versioning with lifecycle policies that expire non-current versions after N days gives you data protection without paying to store every version indefinitely.

children.title: S3 Cross-Region Replication
children.description: S3 Cross-Region Replication (CRR) automatically copies objects to a bucket in a different AWS Region, supporting disaster recovery, geographic data sovereignty, and latency reduction for globally distributed users. Replication can be scoped to specific prefixes or tags, allowing granular control over what data is replicated rather than blindly copying everything. S3 Replication Time Control (RTC) provides a guaranteed 99.99 percent of objects replication within 15 minutes, which is necessary for strict RPO targets.

resources.label: S3 Replication Deep Dive – AWS Blog

aws-architect.title: Security and Identity
aws-architect.description: Implement defense-in-depth security using AWS native security services. Apply the principle of least privilege and encrypt data at rest and in transit.
aws-architect.challenge: Set up a security baseline with CloudTrail enabled in all regions, a Config rule that alerts on public S3 buckets, and GuardDuty findings forwarded to a security SNS topic.

children.title: Permission Boundaries and SCPs
children.description: IAM permission boundaries cap the maximum permissions an identity can exercise even if their attached policies would otherwise allow more, making them essential for safely delegating IAM management to development teams without risk of privilege escalation. Service Control Policies (SCPs) applied through AWS Organizations enforce organization-wide guardrails that no individual account can override, such as preventing deployment outside approved regions. Together, boundaries and SCPs enable a federated security model where teams have autonomy within clearly defined guardrails.

children.title: AWS Organizations and Account Strategy
children.description: AWS Organizations allows you to manage multiple AWS accounts as a single entity, applying policies centrally and consolidating billing for volume discounts. A multi-account strategy is the AWS-recommended approach for large organizations because it provides strong isolation boundaries between environments and business units—a compromised account cannot affect neighboring accounts. The typical structure uses separate accounts for production, staging, development, security tooling, and shared services, managed through organizational units.

children.title: AWS KMS Key Management
children.description: AWS Key Management Service (KMS) provides a FIPS 140-2 validated hardware-backed key store for creating and managing encryption keys used by dozens of AWS services. Customer Managed Keys (CMKs) give you full control over key policies, rotation, and deletion, while AWS Managed Keys are automatically managed but offer less control for compliance requirements. Every architect must understand KMS key policies versus IAM policies, as both must grant access before a key can be used, and misconfiguration is a common source of access-denied errors in encrypted workloads.

children.title: Secrets Manager and Parameter Store
children.description: AWS Secrets Manager stores and automatically rotates credentials for databases, APIs, and third-party services, eliminating hardcoded secrets in application code or environment variables. Lambda rotation functions execute on a configurable schedule to refresh secrets before they expire, and Secrets Manager updates the secret value without requiring application restarts. Systems Manager Parameter Store provides a simpler and cheaper alternative for non-sensitive configuration values, and it integrates natively with EC2, ECS, and Lambda for secure configuration injection.

children.title: AWS GuardDuty
children.description: GuardDuty is a machine-learning-powered threat detection service that continuously analyzes CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs to identify malicious activity like crypto-mining, compromised credentials, and data exfiltration patterns. It requires no agents, no configuration, and no infrastructure to maintain, making it one of the fastest security controls to enable across an entire organization through AWS Organizations. GuardDuty findings integrate with Security Hub for centralized alerting and with EventBridge for automated remediation workflows.

children.title: CloudTrail and AWS Config
children.description: CloudTrail records every API call made in your AWS account, providing the audit trail required for forensic investigation, compliance reporting, and troubleshooting infrastructure changes. AWS Config continuously records the configuration state of AWS resources and evaluates them against desired-state rules, detecting drift and compliance violations in real time. Together, CloudTrail answers who did what and when, while Config answers what did the resource look like at any point in time.

children.title: AWS WAF and Shield
children.description: AWS WAF protects web applications from common exploits like SQL injection and cross-site scripting at the edge by inspecting HTTP/S requests before they reach your application. AWS Shield Standard is automatically included at no extra cost and defends against the most common volumetric DDoS attacks, while Shield Advanced provides 24/7 DDoS response team support and cost protection for scaling during attacks. Deploying WAF rules using managed rule groups from AWS or the AWS Marketplace gets you expert threat signatures without writing rules from scratch.

children.title: Security Hub and Compliance
children.description: AWS Security Hub aggregates security findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, and third-party tools into a single prioritized view mapped to compliance standards like CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark and PCI DSS. Its automated security checks run continuously and score your environment against hundreds of controls, giving you an actionable compliance posture rather than a one-time audit snapshot. Integrating Security Hub with EventBridge enables automatic remediation workflows for critical findings without manual intervention.

aws-architect.title: Infrastructure as Code with CloudFormation
aws-architect.description: Define and manage AWS infrastructure declaratively using CloudFormation templates and the AWS CDK. Implement change sets, nested stacks, and multi-account deployments.
aws-architect.challenge: Create a CloudFormation stack set that deploys a VPC, bastion host, and S3 logging bucket across three AWS accounts with a single command.

children.title: Template Anatomy and Intrinsic Functions
children.description: A CloudFormation template is a declarative JSON or YAML document that describes the desired state of AWS resources, and mastering intrinsic functions like Ref, Fn::Sub, Fn::If, and Fn::GetAtt is what separates a beginner template from a production-grade one. These functions allow dynamic value resolution at deploy time, enabling templates that adapt to different environments without code duplication. Understanding the template evaluation order—parameters resolved first, then conditions, then resources—prevents circular dependency errors during stack creation.

children.title: Nested Stacks and Cross-Stack References
children.description: Nested stacks decompose large CloudFormation templates into smaller, reusable modules that can be versioned and shared independently, reducing duplication across environments and teams. Cross-stack references using Outputs and ImportValue allow stacks to consume values from other stacks without hardcoding resource IDs, enabling loose coupling between infrastructure components. Knowing when to use nested stacks versus cross-stack references versus StackSets is a key architectural decision that affects how easily infrastructure can be refactored later.

children.title: Change Sets and Rollback Triggers
children.description: CloudFormation Change Sets preview the exact resource actions a stack update will perform before execution, serving as a critical safety gate that prevents accidental deletions or replacements of stateful resources like databases. Rollback Triggers allow you to specify CloudWatch alarms that, if breached during a stack update, cause CloudFormation to automatically roll back all changes, acting as automated regression tests for infrastructure. Combining change set review with rollback triggers creates a safe continuous delivery pipeline for infrastructure changes.

children.title: Drift Detection and Remediation
children.description: CloudFormation Drift Detection compares the actual configuration of stack resources against the expected configuration defined in the template, flagging resources that have been modified outside of CloudFormation by manual console changes or CLI commands. Undetected drift is dangerous because the next stack update may overwrite manual changes or fail due to unexpected resource states. Building a regular drift detection schedule into operational runbooks ensures that IaC remains the source of truth for infrastructure state.

children.title: CDK Constructs and Apps
children.description: The AWS Cloud Development Kit (CDK) allows you to define cloud infrastructure using familiar programming languages like TypeScript, Python, and Java, which enables unit testing, IDE autocompletion, and code reuse patterns that YAML templates cannot provide. CDK constructs exist at three levels: L1 constructs mirror CloudFormation resources, L2 constructs add sensible defaults and helper methods, and L3 constructs encode complete patterns like a VPC with standard subnets. Using CDK Aspects allows you to enforce organization-wide compliance rules across all stacks declaratively in code.

children.title: CDK Pipelines for CI/CD
children.description: CDK Pipelines is a high-level construct library that creates a self-mutating CodePipeline for deploying CDK applications, meaning the pipeline itself updates when you change the pipeline stack definition. It enforces a standard multi-stage deployment pattern with manual approval gates between environments, which is the production standard for safely promoting infrastructure changes through dev, staging, and prod. Self-mutation is a killer feature because it means infrastructure changes to the pipeline itself are deployed safely rather than requiring manual intervention.

children.title: CloudFormation StackSets
children.description: StackSets extend CloudFormation to deploy stacks across multiple accounts and regions with a single operation, making them essential for enforcing baseline configurations like CloudTrail, GuardDuty, and security group rules across an entire AWS Organization. Service-managed StackSets integrate with AWS Organizations to automatically deploy to new accounts as they join an organizational unit, enabling zero-touch onboarding. Failure tolerance settings control how many accounts can fail during a deployment before the entire operation is rolled back, balancing speed with safety.

children.title: cfn-lint and Template Validation
children.description: cfn-lint is the official CloudFormation linter that validates templates against the CloudFormation resource specification before deployment, catching type mismatches, deprecated properties, and missing required fields at development time rather than at stack creation. Integrating cfn-lint into CI pipelines provides fast feedback that prevents broken template deployments from reaching environments. CloudFormation Guard (cfn-guard) complements cfn-lint by evaluating templates against custom compliance rules, enabling teams to enforce organizational policies like mandatory encryption or approved AMI lists.

aws-architect.title: Serverless and Managed Services
aws-architect.description: Integrate serverless compute with managed messaging and API services to build event-driven architectures. Understand throttling, dead-letter queues, and fan-out patterns.
topics: API Gateway REST and HTTP APIs
aws-architect.challenge: Build an order processing pipeline using API Gateway, Lambda, SQS, and a Step Functions workflow that handles failures and sends status notifications via SNS.

children.title: Lambda Triggers and Event Sources
children.description: Lambda integrates natively with over 200 AWS services as event sources, enabling you to build systems where code runs only in response to events like S3 uploads, DynamoDB stream changes, SQS messages, or API Gateway requests. The event source mapping for SQS provides built-in batching and retry logic, automatically handling message visibility timeouts and sending failed messages to a dead-letter queue after exhausting retries. Choosing the right trigger type for each use case is fundamental to serverless architecture, as some sources support synchronous invocation while others are asynchronous with different failure semantics.

children.title: Lambda Layers and Dependencies
children.description: Lambda Layers provide a mechanism to share common dependencies and utility code across multiple functions without including them in each deployment package, which reduces package size and simplifies updates to shared libraries. A shared layer containing the AWS SDK, database drivers, or internal SDKs can be updated in one place and picked up by all functions that reference it on next invocation. Layers also enable separation of concerns by allowing security teams to inject approved security libraries as a mandatory layer without modifying individual function code.

children.title: SQS Standard and FIFO Queues
children.description: Amazon SQS decouples producers and consumers, allowing each to scale independently and absorb traffic spikes without losing messages. Standard queues offer maximum throughput but allow out-of-order delivery and at-least-once processing, requiring consumers to be idempotent. FIFO queues guarantee exactly-once processing and strict ordering within a message group, necessary for financial transactions or order pipelines where duplication or re-ordering would corrupt business logic.

resources.label: SQS Standard vs FIFO Queue Comparison

children.title: SNS Fan-Out Pattern
children.description: Amazon SNS enables the fan-out messaging pattern where a single published message is delivered simultaneously to multiple SQS queues, Lambda functions, or HTTP endpoints, enabling parallel downstream processing without tight coupling between services. This pattern is commonly used for order processing where one event triggers inventory updates, email notifications, and analytics ingestion simultaneously. SNS message filtering policies allow individual subscribers to receive only the subset of messages they care about, reducing unnecessary processing across the fleet.

children.title: REST vs HTTP APIs
children.description: API Gateway offers two main API types: REST APIs with full feature sets including caching, request/response transformation, and AWS service integrations, and HTTP APIs that are significantly cheaper and faster but with a reduced feature set. HTTP APIs are the preferred choice for most Lambda-backed APIs due to their lower latency, lower cost, and native JWT authorizer support. REST APIs remain necessary when you need request validation, usage plans, API keys, or direct service integrations with AWS services like DynamoDB or SQS.

resources.label: REST API vs HTTP API Comparison
API Gateway HTTP APIs Deep Dive

children.title: API Gateway Throttling and Caching
children.description: API Gateway throttling limits the rate of incoming requests at the stage and method level to protect your backend from traffic surges, and burst limits and steady-state rate limits are configured independently to handle spikes gracefully. The API Gateway response cache stores backend responses for a configurable TTL, dramatically reducing Lambda invocations and database queries for idempotent endpoints. Properly sizing the cache capacity and choosing which endpoints to cache versus which must always hit the backend is a significant performance and cost optimization decision.

children.title: EventBridge Rules and Buses
children.description: Amazon EventBridge is a serverless event bus that enables event-driven architectures where services publish events and subscribers react without direct coupling. Custom event buses allow you to isolate event streams by domain, and partner event sources enable ingestion of third-party SaaS events directly into your AWS architecture. EventBridge Pipes connect event sources to targets with optional filtering and enrichment, eliminating the need for a Lambda function that simply passes events through.

children.title: Step Functions State Machines
children.description: AWS Step Functions orchestrates multi-step workflows as state machines, providing built-in retry logic, error handling, parallel execution, and human approval steps that would require complex custom code if implemented inside Lambda. Standard workflows are ideal for long-running processes that need durable execution history, while Express workflows are optimized for high-volume, short-duration workflows at a fraction of the cost. Using Step Functions service integrations to call DynamoDB, ECS, or SageMaker directly—without an intermediate Lambda—reduces latency and eliminates unnecessary costs.

resources.label: Step Functions Use Cases and Patterns

aws-architect.title: High Availability and Disaster Recovery
aws-architect.description: Design systems that meet RTO and RPO targets using AWS global services and cross-region replication strategies. Implement failover routing and content distribution.
topics: Route 53 routing policies and health checks
aws-architect.challenge: Implement a multi-region active-passive setup with Route 53 health-check-based failover, cross-region RDS read replica promotion, and CloudFront for static assets.

children.title: Route 53 Routing Policies
children.description: Route 53's routing policies give architects precise control over how DNS queries are answered—failover routing automatically redirects traffic to a secondary endpoint when the primary fails health checks, while latency-based routing directs users to the AWS region with the lowest latency. Weighted routing enables gradual traffic shifting between environments for blue-green deployments at the DNS level without deploying new infrastructure. Understanding the health check TTL and DNS propagation delay is critical when designing RTO targets, because DNS-based failover cannot switch traffic faster than the record's TTL.

children.title: Route 53 Health Checks
children.description: Route 53 Health Checks monitor endpoints and CloudWatch alarms to automatically detect failures and trigger failover routing policies without human intervention. Calculated health checks aggregate the status of multiple child health checks, enabling complex health logic like traffic failover when more than half of your fleet is unhealthy rather than all of it. Configuring health checks with appropriate request intervals and failure thresholds balances detection speed against false positives from momentary network blips.

children.title: CloudFront Distributions and Caching
children.description: Amazon CloudFront is a global CDN with over 450 points of presence that caches content close to users, reducing latency and offloading origin servers. Cache behaviors define per-path caching rules including TTLs, allowed HTTP methods, and forwarded headers, allowing granular control over what gets cached versus what always passes through to origin. CloudFront Origin Shield adds an additional caching layer in front of the origin, reducing origin load by up to 73 percent for globally distributed user bases.

children.title: AWS Global Accelerator
children.description: AWS Global Accelerator routes TCP and UDP traffic through the AWS global network backbone rather than the public internet, providing up to 60 percent improvement in throughput and reduced jitter for latency-sensitive applications. Unlike CloudFront, which caches HTTP content at edge locations, Global Accelerator provides network-level routing without caching—making it ideal for non-cacheable workloads like gaming, IoT, and financial applications. Static Anycast IP addresses provided by Global Accelerator eliminate IP address changes during failover, which would otherwise invalidate client-side IP allowlists.

children.title: RTO, RPO, and DR Patterns
children.description: Recovery Time Objective (RTO) and Recovery Point Objective (RPO) are the two key metrics that drive disaster recovery architecture decisions—RTO defines how long you can be down, and RPO defines how much data you can afford to lose. The four AWS DR patterns—backup/restore, pilot light, warm standby, and multi-site active/active—represent a spectrum from lowest cost/highest RTO to highest cost/lowest RTO. Matching your architecture to your contractual SLAs rather than over-engineering to the most expensive pattern is a key architectural judgment.

children.title: AWS Backup and Cross-Region Replication
children.description: AWS Backup provides a unified backup service that manages backup schedules, retention policies, and cross-region copy for EC2, EBS, RDS, DynamoDB, EFS, and more, replacing fragmented per-service backup configurations. Backup Vaults can be locked to prevent deletion by even the root account, satisfying regulatory requirements for immutable backup storage. Backup Plans with cross-region copy rules automate the creation of geographically isolated recovery points without manual operations.

children.title: AWS Fault Injection Simulator
children.description: AWS Fault Injection Simulator (FIS) is a managed service for running controlled chaos experiments on AWS infrastructure, testing how your system responds to realistic failure conditions like EC2 instance termination, AZ degradation, or network latency injection. Running FIS experiments in production—with appropriate safety stops configured—reveals resilience gaps that only appear under real failure conditions, which staged testing environments cannot replicate. Documenting experiment hypotheses, results, and remediation actions transforms chaos engineering from an activity into a repeatable operational practice.

children.title: Resilience Testing Best Practices
children.description: Resilience testing goes beyond DR drills to continuously verify that your system meets its availability targets under realistic failure conditions, including network partitions, dependency outages, and data corruption. Defining game-day scenarios with explicit success criteria before running experiments prevents confirmation bias and ensures findings are actionable. Integrating FIS experiments into CI/CD pipelines as automated gates catches regressions in resiliency before infrastructure changes reach production.

aws-architect.title: Cost Optimization and Performance
aws-architect.description: Apply the AWS Well-Architected cost optimization pillar to identify waste and right-size resources. Use savings plans, reserved instances, and spot strategically.
topics: Spot instance strategies and interruption handling
aws-architect.challenge: Audit a simulated AWS account, identify the top three cost drivers, implement savings plan purchases, set up cost allocation tags, and create budget alerts for each team.

children.title: AWS Cost Explorer and Budgets
children.description: AWS Cost Explorer provides visual tools for analyzing spending patterns across services, linked accounts, and tags, enabling engineers to quickly identify what changed month-over-month and why. Cost allocation tags are a prerequisite for meaningful cost breakdown because untagged resources appear as undifferentiated spend that cannot be attributed to teams or products. AWS Budgets adds proactive spend controls by sending alerts and triggering actions like IAM policy attachments or EC2 fleet shutdowns when forecasted spend approaches defined thresholds.

children.title: Cost Anomaly Detection
children.description: AWS Cost Anomaly Detection uses machine learning to identify unusual spend patterns relative to your historical baseline, alerting you to unexpected cost spikes within hours rather than at month-end billing review. It reduces the manual overhead of monitoring hundreds of cost dimensions by learning what normal looks like for each AWS service and flagging statistically significant deviations. Configuring anomaly monitors per linked account, service, or cost category allows different teams to receive targeted alerts relevant to their area of responsibility.

children.title: Savings Plans Strategy
children.description: AWS Savings Plans offer discounts of up to 66 percent in exchange for a commitment to a consistent amount of compute usage (measured in $/hour) over one or three years, with Compute Savings Plans automatically applying to any EC2, Lambda, or Fargate usage regardless of region or instance family. Purchasing too aggressively locks you into more commitment than your usage warrants, while under-purchasing leaves money on the table by paying On-Demand prices. The optimal coverage target is typically 70–80 percent of your stable baseline usage, with On-Demand covering the unpredictable remainder.

children.title: Reserved Instances and Convertible RIs
children.description: Reserved Instances provide the deepest discounts for specific EC2 instance types in specific regions, making them ideal for long-running workloads with stable and predictable resource profiles like production databases and application servers. Convertible Reserved Instances sacrifice some discount depth in exchange for the ability to exchange the RI for a different instance type or family, providing a safety net if your instance type requirements change during the commitment term. The RI Marketplace allows you to sell unused Reserved Instances to other AWS customers, providing an exit for over-purchased commitments.

children.title: AWS Compute Optimizer
children.description: AWS Compute Optimizer analyzes CloudWatch metrics from EC2 instances, Auto Scaling Groups, EBS volumes, Lambda functions, and ECS services to generate machine-learning-based right-sizing recommendations that identify over-provisioned resources. Following Compute Optimizer recommendations for instances running at less than 40 percent average CPU utilization can reduce EC2 spend by 25–40 percent without degrading application performance. Compute Optimizer Enhanced Infrastructure Metrics uses 3 months of metrics history rather than 14 days, producing more accurate recommendations for seasonal workloads.

children.title: S3 Intelligent Tiering
children.description: S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves objects between multiple access tiers—Frequent Access, Infrequent Access, Archive Instant Access, and Deep Archive—based on actual access patterns, eliminating the need to manually predict and configure lifecycle rules. It is the optimal storage class for data with unknown or unpredictable access patterns because there are no retrieval fees for objects in the Frequent and Infrequent Access tiers. The monitoring and automation fee (approximately 0.0025 per 1,000 objects per month) is the only overhead, which pays for itself once objects are moved to a lower-cost tier.

children.title: ElastiCache for Caching
children.description: Amazon ElastiCache provides managed Redis and Memcached clusters that sit between your application and database, serving frequently requested data from in-memory cache at sub-millisecond latency. Caching database query results, user sessions, and computed values reduces database load, lowers RDS costs, and dramatically improves application response times under high read traffic. Cache eviction policy, TTL strategy, and cache invalidation logic are the three design decisions that determine whether a cache improves or silently corrupts application data.

children.title: AWS Trusted Advisor
children.description: AWS Trusted Advisor continuously scans your AWS environment across five categories—cost optimization, performance, security, fault tolerance, and service limits—and provides prioritized recommendations to act on. The cost optimization checks alone typically surface thousands of dollars in monthly savings opportunities by identifying idle EC2 instances, unattociated Elastic IPs, and underutilized EBS volumes. Business and Enterprise support tiers unlock the full set of checks and provide programmatic access to findings via the Trusted Advisor API for automated remediation workflows.

aws-architect.title: Advanced Architecture and AWS Professional
aws-architect.description: Design enterprise-grade multi-account AWS landing zones with governance, compliance, and self-service capabilities. Apply all five Well-Architected pillars at scale.
topics: AWS Landing Zone and Control Tower
AWS Service Catalog products and portfolios

resources.label: AWS Solutions Architect Professional Exam Guide
AWS Certified Solutions Architect Study Guide

aws-architect.challenge: Design a full enterprise landing zone with Control Tower, three organizational units, a shared services VPC connected via Transit Gateway, and a Service Catalog portfolio for developer self-service.

children.title: AWS Control Tower
children.description: AWS Control Tower automates the setup of a secure, multi-account AWS environment based on AWS best practices, creating an organizational structure, baseline guardrails, and an Account Factory for provisioning new accounts consistently. It enforces preventive guardrails via SCPs and detective guardrails via AWS Config rules, ensuring every account in the organization starts from a compliant baseline. Customizations for AWS Control Tower (CfCT) allow organizations to extend the landing zone with additional account-level configurations applied automatically at account vending time.

resources.label: AWS Control Tower Deep Dive – re:Invent

children.title: AWS Service Catalog
children.description: AWS Service Catalog allows platform teams to create and manage approved portfolios of CloudFormation-based products that developers can self-service provision without needing direct IAM access to create arbitrary AWS resources. Constraining what resources developers can create—enforcing instance types, VPC configurations, and security settings—prevents shadow IT and ensures every workload meets organizational standards. Service Catalog AppRegistry connects deployed products to application metadata, enabling cost allocation, compliance tracking, and operational visibility across the entire application portfolio.

children.title: AWS Transit Gateway
children.description: AWS Transit Gateway acts as a regional network hub that interconnects hundreds of VPCs and on-premises networks through a single gateway, replacing the O(n²) complexity of full-mesh VPC peering with a hub-and-spoke model. Route tables in Transit Gateway control which attachments can communicate with each other, enabling segmentation between production, development, and shared services VPCs while maintaining central network control. Transit Gateway Inter-Region peering extends this hub-and-spoke model globally, connecting regional hubs without data traversing the public internet.

children.title: AWS Direct Connect
children.description: AWS Direct Connect provides dedicated private network connections from your on-premises data center to AWS, delivering consistent network performance and lower latency than internet-based VPN connections, critical for hybrid architectures with high-throughput data transfer. Direct Connect Gateways allow a single Direct Connect connection to access multiple AWS Regions and VPCs without establishing separate connections per destination. For maximum resilience, the recommended pattern is two Direct Connect connections from different providers to different AWS Direct Connect locations, with a Site-to-Site VPN as a backup path.

children.title: Well-Architected Tool and Reviews
children.description: The AWS Well-Architected Tool provides a structured review process against the six pillars of the Well-Architected Framework, producing a prioritized list of high-risk issues with prescriptive remediation guidance. Conducting Well-Architected Reviews for every production workload at least annually identifies drift from best practices that accumulates as systems evolve. Custom lenses extend the framework with organization-specific questions for domain-specific concerns like data sovereignty, internal security policies, or industry regulations.

children.title: AWS Outposts and Hybrid Architecture
children.description: AWS Outposts brings native AWS hardware and services to on-premises locations, enabling workloads that require data residency, ultra-low latency to on-premises systems, or local data processing to run on the same AWS APIs and control plane used in the cloud. Architects choose Outposts when regulatory requirements mandate that data never leave a specific facility, or when local processing latency requirements cannot be met from a nearby AWS Region. Designing for Outposts requires planning for the Local Gateway (LGW) for local on-premises routing and understanding the service subset available on-premises versus in the Region.

children.title: AWS Solutions Architect Professional Exam
children.description: The AWS Certified Solutions Architect – Professional certification validates the ability to design complex and scalable systems on AWS covering all the topics in this roadmap plus advanced networking, migration strategies, and cost optimization at enterprise scale. The exam requires designing architectures across multiple AWS accounts and services with realistic constraints like budget limits, compliance requirements, and migration timelines. Consistent practice with scenario-based questions that have no clearly wrong answers—only better and worse trade-offs—is the most effective preparation strategy.

children.title: Architecture Decision Records
children.description: Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) document the context, decision, and consequences of significant architectural choices, creating institutional memory that survives team turnover and enables future architects to understand why the system is designed the way it is. Well-written ADRs capture the options that were considered and rejected, which is often more valuable than just the chosen option because it prevents relitigating decisions that were already made with full context. Adopting ADRs as a team practice transforms architecture from tribal knowledge held by a few senior engineers into documented organizational knowledge.

azure-architect.title: Azure Fundamentals
azure-architect.description: Understand Azure global infrastructure, core services, and the Azure Resource Manager model. Navigate the portal, CLI, and PowerShell to provision and manage resources.
topics: Azure Free Tier and pricing calculator
azure-architect.challenge: Create a resource group, deploy a Linux VM, attach a managed disk, and configure a network security group to allow SSH only from your IP.

children.title: Regions and Availability Zones
children.description: Azure operates 60+ regions worldwide, and understanding which region to deploy to directly impacts latency, compliance, and disaster recovery. Availability zones are physically separate datacenters within a region, each with independent power, cooling, and networking, giving you 99.99% SLA on VMs. Choosing the wrong region can mean violating data residency laws or adding hundreds of milliseconds of latency to end users.

children.title: Management Groups and Subscriptions
children.description: Management groups provide a governance scope above subscriptions, allowing you to apply Azure Policy and RBAC across multiple subscriptions simultaneously. A well-designed hierarchy — tenant root, platform, landing zones, sandbox — establishes the guardrails that every team in the organization operates within. Without this structure, each subscription becomes an isolated island with inconsistent compliance and no centralized cost visibility.

children.title: ARM Model and Resource Groups
children.description: Every Azure resource lives inside a Resource Group, and all operations go through the Azure Resource Manager API, which enforces RBAC, locks, and tags consistently. Resource groups are the unit of deployment and lifecycle management — resources in the same group share a common lifecycle and can be deleted together. Understanding the ARM model is foundational because every tool, from the portal to Bicep to Terraform, talks to the same underlying API.

resources.label: Azure Resource Manager — John Savill's Technical Training

children.title: Azure CLI and PowerShell Basics
children.description: The Azure CLI and Az PowerShell module let you automate resource provisioning, querying, and management without touching the portal, which is essential for repeatable and scriptable workflows. Knowing both tools means you can operate in any environment — Linux pipelines tend to favor the CLI, while Windows-centric teams often lean on PowerShell. Mastering these tools early eliminates manual portal work and is a prerequisite for writing automation and IaC.

children.title: Virtual Machines and Managed Disks
children.description: Azure Virtual Machines are the foundational IaaS building block and understanding instance families — compute-optimized, memory-optimized, GPU — lets you match workload requirements to the right hardware without overspending. Managed disks abstract storage account management and offer Premium SSD, Ultra Disk, and Standard HDD tiers, each with different IOPS and latency guarantees. Getting VM sizing right from the start avoids the expensive anti-pattern of over-provisioned instances running at 5% CPU.

children.title: Azure Pricing Calculator
children.description: Estimating costs before deploying prevents bill shock and allows you to present business cases for cloud projects with real numbers. The pricing calculator lets you model multiple scenarios — reserved vs. pay-as-you-go, different regions, different SKUs — so you can find the most cost-efficient configuration before any resource is provisioned. Architects who skip this step routinely face budget overruns that erode trust with stakeholders.

resources.label: Azure Total Cost of Ownership Calculator

children.title: AZ-900 Microsoft Azure Fundamentals
children.description: The AZ-900 exam validates foundational knowledge of cloud concepts, core Azure services, security, privacy, compliance, and Azure pricing and support. It is the recommended starting point for anyone entering the Azure ecosystem because it builds the shared vocabulary used across all other Azure certifications. Passing it demonstrates credibility to employers and establishes the conceptual framework on which all advanced Azure topics build.

children.title: Microsoft Learn Sandbox
children.description: Microsoft Learn provides free, time-limited sandbox environments that let you practice Azure without needing your own subscription or incurring charges, removing the biggest barrier to hands-on learning. The interactive exercises are tied directly to the learning module content, ensuring that theory and practice are always connected. Using the sandbox to follow along with modules accelerates retention compared to reading alone.

azure-architect.title: Azure Networking
azure-architect.description: Design Azure virtual networks with proper segmentation, routing, and connectivity. Understand hybrid connectivity options including VPN Gateway and ExpressRoute.
topics: Network security groups and application security groups
Azure Load Balancer and Application Gateway
Azure DNS public and private zones
azure-architect.challenge: Build a hub-and-spoke network topology with a shared services hub VNet, two spoke VNets connected via peering, and an Application Gateway in the hub for inbound traffic.

children.title: VNet Address Spaces and Subnets
children.description: Choosing non-overlapping CIDR blocks for virtual networks is critical because overlapping ranges block VNet peering and create routing conflicts that are expensive to fix after workloads are deployed. Subnets are the segmentation unit within a VNet and each subnet can have its own NSG and route table, enabling you to apply different trust zones to application tiers. Planning your IP address space with room to grow prevents forced re-IP operations as environments expand.

children.title: Network Security Groups and ASGs
children.description: NSGs are stateful L4 firewalls attached to subnets or NICs that filter inbound and outbound traffic using priority-ordered rules, and they are the primary mechanism for network-layer microsegmentation in Azure. Application Security Groups let you reference logical workload groups in NSG rules instead of individual IP addresses, which makes rules far more maintainable as workloads scale horizontally. Combining NSGs on subnets with ASGs on NICs provides defense-in-depth at both the network and resource level.

children.title: Azure Load Balancer vs Application Gateway
children.description: Azure Load Balancer operates at L4 for ultra-low latency TCP/UDP distribution within a region, while Application Gateway operates at L7, enabling cookie-based session affinity, SSL termination, URL path routing, and WAF integration. Choosing the wrong layer means either over-paying for L7 capabilities on internal traffic that only needs L4, or missing WAF protection on internet-facing endpoints. Understanding when each is appropriate is a core Azure networking skill.

children.title: Azure DNS and Private Zones
children.description: Azure DNS private zones provide name resolution within virtual networks without exposing DNS records to the public internet, enabling service discovery for VMs and Private Endpoints without custom DNS servers. Linking a private zone to multiple VNets with auto-registration means new VMs automatically get DNS records, eliminating manual host file management. Misconfigured DNS is one of the most common sources of mysterious connectivity failures in Azure environments.

children.title: VPN Gateway Site-to-Site
children.description: Site-to-site VPN Gateway creates an encrypted IPsec/IKE tunnel between your on-premises network and Azure VNet over the public internet, providing a cost-effective path for hybrid workloads that don't require guaranteed bandwidth. Active-active VPN Gateway configurations with BGP provide redundancy and automatic failover, eliminating the gateway itself as a single point of failure. For proof-of-concept or low-bandwidth scenarios it is the fastest route from on-premises to Azure.

children.title: ExpressRoute Circuits and Peering
children.description: ExpressRoute provides dedicated private connectivity to Azure that bypasses the public internet, offering SLA-backed bandwidth from 50 Mbps to 100 Gbps with lower and more predictable latency than VPN. ExpressRoute Global Reach lets you interconnect on-premises sites through the Microsoft backbone, which is often cheaper and faster than routing traffic through your own WAN. For regulated industries or latency-sensitive workloads, ExpressRoute is frequently a compliance and performance requirement rather than a nice-to-have.

children.title: VNet Peering and Transitive Routing
children.description: VNet peering connects virtual networks with low-latency, high-bandwidth Microsoft backbone routing but peering is non-transitive by default, meaning spoke-to-spoke traffic must route through a hub NVA or Azure Firewall. This non-transitive behavior is the most common architecture surprise for engineers moving from on-premises networks, and not accounting for it leads to hairpin routing scenarios that add latency and cost. Azure Virtual WAN solves this with any-to-any routing built in, which is worth evaluating for large hub-and-spoke deployments.

children.title: Azure Firewall for Centralized Egress
children.description: Azure Firewall is a stateful managed firewall service built into Azure that centralizes outbound internet traffic inspection, FQDN-based filtering, and threat intelligence-based blocking for all spokes through a single hub. Its managed nature means Microsoft handles patching and scaling, and its deep integration with Azure Monitor makes logging and alerting straightforward. Centralizing egress through Azure Firewall is a foundational pattern in the Azure landing zone architecture.

resources.label: Azure Firewall vs NSG — Architecture Decision

azure-architect.title: Identity and Access with Entra ID
azure-architect.description: Implement enterprise identity management using Microsoft Entra ID including conditional access policies and privileged identity management. Federate external identity providers and manage app registrations.
topics: Entra ID tenants users and groups
Role-based access control and custom roles
azure-architect.challenge: Configure a conditional access policy requiring MFA for admins, enable PIM for the Contributor role in a subscription, and set up a managed identity for a VM to access Key Vault.

children.title: Tenants, Users, and Groups
children.description: A Microsoft Entra ID tenant is the identity backbone for every Azure resource, and understanding how users, groups, and service principals relate is essential before assigning any permissions. Groups are the primary vehicle for scalable RBAC — assigning roles to groups rather than individual users means onboarding and offboarding require only group membership changes. Nested groups and dynamic membership rules based on user attributes further reduce manual identity management overhead.

resources.label: Manage Users and Groups in Entra ID — Microsoft Learn

children.title: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
children.description: Azure RBAC is the authorization system for controlling who can do what on which Azure resources, and it operates at management group, subscription, resource group, or individual resource scope. Built-in roles like Reader, Contributor, and Owner cover most scenarios, but custom roles allow precise permission sets when built-ins are either too broad or too narrow. Enforcing least-privilege RBAC is the single most impactful security control in Azure because overly permissive roles are the root cause of the majority of cloud security incidents.

children.title: Conditional Access Policies
children.description: Conditional access is the modern security policy engine in Entra ID that evaluates signals — user, location, device, app, risk — and enforces access controls like MFA, compliant device requirement, or block. Implementing conditional access is the most effective way to stop credential-stuffing attacks because even stolen passwords are useless without a compliant device or a passed MFA challenge. Zero Trust architecture is built on the principle that no access is granted without continuous evaluation of these signals.

children.title: Privileged Identity Management (PIM)
children.description: PIM provides just-in-time privileged access, requiring administrators to activate elevated roles for a time-bounded window with justification and optional approval, rather than holding standing admin rights. This dramatically reduces the attack surface because a compromised admin account that is not currently activated has no elevated Azure permissions to abuse. PIM also provides access reviews that surface stale role assignments, helping keep permissions tight over time.

children.title: App Registrations and OAuth 2.0
children.description: App registrations in Entra ID are the identity representation of applications that need to authenticate users or access Azure resources, and understanding OAuth 2.0 flows — authorization code, client credentials, device code — is necessary to integrate any application securely. Misconfigurations like overly broad API permissions or missing redirect URI validation are common sources of OAuth-based attacks. Every custom application deployed on Azure should use an app registration rather than credentials embedded in code.

children.title: Managed Identities for Azure Resources
children.description: Managed identities eliminate the need to store credentials in application code by giving Azure resources a system-assigned or user-assigned identity that can authenticate to any Entra-ID-aware service including Key Vault, Storage, and SQL. This removes the entire class of credential leakage vulnerabilities because there are no secrets to leak — Azure handles token issuance and rotation automatically. Managed identities are the recommended authentication mechanism for all workloads running on Azure compute.

children.title: Entra External ID and B2B Collaboration
children.description: Entra External ID (formerly Azure AD B2B) allows you to invite external users into your tenant and grant them controlled access to resources without managing their credentials, using their own organization's identity provider. This is the right pattern for partner collaboration scenarios — contractors, vendors, auditors — because it keeps authentication responsibility with the external organization while you control authorization within your tenant. Using B2B instead of creating guest accounts with passwords removes credential management overhead and improves security posture.

children.title: Entra ID Protection and Risk Policies
children.description: Entra ID Protection uses machine learning to detect risky sign-ins and compromised users, and risk-based conditional access policies can automatically require step-up authentication or block access when risk is detected in real time. Enabling identity protection provides a second line of defense when conditional access policies cannot prevent an initial authentication, such as from a known credential leak. Organizations that enable ID Protection and risk-based policies see significantly lower rates of successful account takeover.

resources.label: What is Microsoft Entra ID Protection?

azure-architect.title: Compute and Containers
azure-architect.description: Deploy and scale workloads across Azure virtual machine scale sets, App Service plans, and Azure Kubernetes Service. Choose the right compute abstraction for each workload pattern.
topics: Azure App Service tiers and deployment slots
AKS cluster creation and node pools
Azure Functions consumption vs premium plans
azure-architect.challenge: Deploy a containerized API to AKS with horizontal pod autoscaling, a private container registry with managed identity pull access, and an ingress controller with TLS.

children.title: VMSS Autoscaling and Flexible Orchestration
children.description: VM Scale Sets with Flexible orchestration allow you to mix instance types within the same scale set, spread instances across availability zones, and apply autoscale rules based on CPU, memory, or custom Application Insights metrics. The flexible mode is now the recommended approach because it removes the constraints of uniform instance configuration while retaining the management benefits of a scale set. Properly configured autoscale rules prevent both under-provisioning during traffic spikes and waste during idle periods.

children.title: Azure App Service and Deployment Slots
children.description: Azure App Service abstracts the underlying infrastructure for web apps and APIs, providing built-in autoscaling, custom domains, TLS, and CI/CD integrations without managing VMs. Deployment slots enable blue-green deployments where a new version is warmed up in a staging slot before being swapped into production with zero downtime, and the swap can be reversed instantly if issues arise. App Service is typically the right compute choice for standard web workloads because its managed nature significantly reduces operational overhead.

children.title: AKS Cluster Architecture and Node Pools
children.description: AKS manages the Kubernetes control plane for you and lets you create multiple node pools with different VM sizes optimized for specific workloads — CPU-optimized for application pods, GPU nodes for AI inference, spot node pools for batch jobs. System node pools must run on reliable VMs since they host kube-system workloads like CoreDNS and the metrics server, while user node pools can use spot instances to cut costs. Planning your node pool strategy upfront prevents the costly work of draining and migrating workloads later.

children.title: Azure Container Registry and Image Security
children.description: Azure Container Registry (ACR) is the private container registry that integrates natively with AKS, App Service, and Azure DevOps, and its geo-replication feature ensures image availability even during regional outages. ACR Tasks automate image builds triggered by code commits or base image updates, eliminating manual image build and push steps in CI pipelines. Enabling ACR's built-in Microsoft Defender for Containers image scanning ensures vulnerabilities are detected before images reach production.

children.title: Consumption vs Premium Plans
children.description: Azure Functions on the Consumption plan bills per execution and scales to zero, making it ideal for intermittent workloads, while the Premium plan provides pre-warmed instances to eliminate cold starts and VNet integration for private network access. Cold starts on Consumption can add 1–3 seconds of latency for .NET and several seconds for Java, which is unacceptable for latency-sensitive APIs. Choosing the right plan at design time prevents a retrofit later when cold starts start showing up in production monitoring.

children.title: Durable Functions for Stateful Workflows
children.description: Durable Functions extend Azure Functions with stateful orchestration patterns — fan-out/fan-in, human interaction, async HTTP APIs, and eternal monitoring — without managing queues or state storage yourself. The orchestrator function checkpoints its progress to Azure Storage so it can survive restarts without losing work, enabling long-running workflows that execute over minutes, hours, or days. For complex multi-step business processes, Durable Functions eliminate the glue code that would otherwise wire together dozens of queues and storage tables.

children.title: Choosing the Right Azure Compute Service
children.description: Azure offers more than a dozen compute services and choosing the wrong one creates technical debt — running containers on VMs wastes money, running stateful workloads on Functions causes reliability problems. The decision tree considers whether the workload is stateful or stateless, latency-sensitive or batch, containerized or VM-based, event-driven or continuously running. Getting this decision right at design time avoids expensive migrations after deployment.

children.title: Azure Container Instances for On-Demand Workloads
children.description: Azure Container Instances (ACI) provides serverless container execution without a cluster, making it ideal for one-off batch jobs, CI/CD agents, and dev environments that need to spin up and down on demand. ACI containers start in under 30 seconds and are billed per second of CPU and memory consumption, making them extremely cost-efficient for short-lived workloads. Virtual nodes in AKS can burst to ACI during peak demand, giving you the scalability of ACI with the orchestration of Kubernetes.

resources.label: Virtual Nodes with AKS and ACI

azure-architect.title: Storage and Databases
azure-architect.description: Select and configure Azure storage and database services for performance, consistency, and cost. Design geo-redundant and zone-redundant data tiers.
topics: Azure Blob, File, Queue, and Table storage
Cosmos DB APIs and consistency levels
Azure Database for PostgreSQL and MySQL
azure-architect.challenge: Design a data layer with Azure SQL in zone-redundant configuration, Cosmos DB for multi-region writes, and Azure Cache for Redis as a session store with failover configured.

children.title: Blob Storage Tiers and Lifecycle Management
children.description: Azure Blob Storage offers Hot, Cool, Cold, and Archive tiers with a clear trade-off between access cost and storage cost, and lifecycle management policies automate the movement of blobs between tiers based on age. Failing to use lifecycle management on large blob estates results in everything sitting in Hot tier indefinitely, which can be ten times more expensive than the appropriate Cool or Archive tier for data accessed infrequently. Understanding the retrieval latency and rehydration cost of Archive tier is critical for designing cost-effective backup and archival solutions.

children.title: Storage Account Redundancy Options
children.description: Azure Storage redundancy options — LRS, ZRS, GRS, GZRS — define how many copies of data exist, in how many zones, and in how many regions, and they directly determine both the durability SLA and the cost of storage. GZRS provides the highest durability by combining zone redundancy within the primary region with asynchronous replication to a secondary region, and it is the recommended option for production data. Architects who choose LRS for production workloads without understanding the implications create single-zone dependencies that violate most corporate RTO/RPO targets.

children.title: Elastic Pools and Serverless Tier
children.description: Azure SQL elastic pools allow multiple databases to share a pool of DTUs or vCores, which is economical when databases have different peak usage patterns because they all share capacity without each needing to be independently sized for peak. The serverless compute tier goes further by automatically pausing and resuming based on activity, reducing costs for development databases or workloads with unpredictable usage. Choosing between provisioned, serverless, and elastic pools requires analyzing actual usage patterns rather than guessing at peak requirements.

children.title: Zone-Redundant and Geo-Redundant SQL
children.description: Azure SQL Business Critical and General Purpose tiers support zone-redundant configuration that synchronously replicates across availability zones, providing automatic failover within seconds and a 99.995% SLA. Geo-replication and auto-failover groups extend this to cross-region scenarios, enabling a secondary region to take over with minimal data loss when an entire region fails. Designing the right HA configuration upfront is far less expensive than retrofitting it after a production outage exposes the gap.

resources.label: High Availability for Azure SQL Database

children.title: Cosmos DB Consistency Levels
children.description: Cosmos DB provides five tunable consistency levels — Strong, Bounded Staleness, Session, Consistent Prefix, Eventual — that let you trade consistency guarantees for read latency and throughput on a per-request basis. Session consistency is the sweet spot for most applications because it guarantees reads always see your own writes, which eliminates the confusing behavior of eventual consistency without the latency penalty of strong consistency. Misunderstanding consistency levels leads to either over-paying for strong consistency or building applications that show users stale data.

resources.label: Consistency Levels in Azure Cosmos DB

children.title: Cosmos DB Multi-Region Writes
children.description: Enabling multi-region writes in Cosmos DB allows any replica region to accept writes, enabling the closest region to serve both reads and writes simultaneously for globally distributed applications. The Cosmos DB conflict resolution mechanism — last-write-wins or custom merge procedures — handles the write conflicts that arise when multiple regions accept concurrent writes to the same item. For global applications with active users in multiple continents, multi-region writes can reduce P99 write latency by over 50% compared to single-write-region configurations.

children.title: Azure Cache for Redis Patterns
children.description: Azure Cache for Redis is a fully managed Redis service that provides sub-millisecond data retrieval for common patterns — cache-aside, session store, leaderboard, pub/sub — without the operational overhead of self-managing Redis clusters. The cache-aside pattern is the most important to master: the application checks the cache first, populates it on miss, and sets a TTL that balances data freshness with cache hit rate. Database-backed applications that add Redis caching typically see 80–90% reduction in database load for read-heavy workloads.

children.title: Azure Database for PostgreSQL and MySQL
children.description: Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server is the recommended managed relational database for PostgreSQL workloads, offering high availability with a standby in the same zone or a different zone, read replicas, and connection pooling via PgBouncer built in.
